request.question: Both Dario Fo and Raja Rao both recipients of prizes in which area of study?

title: Isabella, Three Sailing Ships and a Con Man
Franca Rame
Raja Rao
Elizabeth: Almost by Chance a Woman
Archangels Don't Play Pinball
Can't Pay? Won't Pay!
The Tale of a Tiger
The Open Couple
Dario Fo
Jacopo Fo
sentences: Isabella, Three Sailing Ships and a Con Man (Italian title: " Isabella, tre caravelle e un cacciaballe") is a 1963 two-act play by Italian playwright Dario Fo, the recipient of the 1997 Nobel Prize in Literature.
Some people got angry: Fo received threatening letters, was assaulted in Rome with Rame by Fascist groups who also threw rubbish at them, while another performance was disrupted by a bomb scare.
He recounted this event in the prologue of "Johan Padan and the Discovery of the Americas".
Franca Rame (18 July 1929 – 29 May 2013) was an Italian theatre actress, playwright and political activist.
She was married to Nobel laureate playwright Dario Fo and is the mother of writer Jacopo Fo.
Fo dedicated his Nobel Prize to her.
Raja Rao (8 November 1908 – 8 July 2006) was an Indian writer of English-language novels and short stories, whose works are deeply rooted in Metaphysics.

"The Serpent and the Rope" (1960), a semi-autobiographical novel recounting a search for spiritual truth in Europe and India, established him as one of the finest Indian prose stylists and won him the Sahitya Akademi Award in 1964.
For the entire body of his work, Rao was awarded the Neustadt International Prize for Literature in 1988.
Rao's wide-ranging body of work, spanning a number of genres, is seen as a varied and significant contribution to Indian English literature, as well as World literature as a whole.
Elizabeth: Almost by Chance a Woman (Italian title: "Quasi per caso una donna: Elisabetta") is a play by Dario Fo written in 1984.
Franca Rame plays Elizabeth I of England, while Fo plays her transvestite cosmetic adviser.
Archangels Don't Play Pinball (Italian title: "Gli arcangeli non giocano al flipper") is a 1959 two-act play by Dario Fo.
The play uses the metaphor of a pinball machine—a new innovation in Italy at the time of and one of which Fo and his wife Franca Rame were fond— to convey mechanisation and conspicuous consumption.
Can't Pay?
Won't Pay!
(Italian: Non Si Paga!
Non Si Paga!
, also translated We Can't Pay?
We Won't Pay!
and Low Pay?
Don't Pay!)
is play originally written in Italian by Dario Fo.
Regarded as Fo's best-known play internationally after "Morte accidentale di un anarchico", it had been performed in 35 countries by 1990.
The Tale of a Tiger (Italian title: "La storia della tigre") is a dramatic monologue by Dario Fo.
Fo collected material for it during a June 1975 visit to China with his wife Franca Rame and other members of their theatre company, and he toured around Italy with it in 1978.
The Open Couple (Italian title: "Coppia aperta, quasi spalancata") is a play by Dario Fo.
As with some of Fo's other plays, it is a romantic play which was written with his wife Franca Rame.
It was written in 1983.

Dario Fo (] ; 24 March 1926 – 13 October 2016) was an Italian actor–playwright, comedian, singer, theatre director, stage designer, songwriter, painter, political campaigner for the Italian left-wing and the recipient of the 1997 Nobel Prize in Literature.
In his time he was "arguably the most widely performed contemporary playwright in world theatre".

Much of his dramatic work depends on improvisation and comprises the recovery of "illegitimate" forms of theatre, such as those performed by "giullari" (medieval strolling players) and, more famously, the ancient Italian style of "commedia dell'arte".
Jacopo Fo (born 31 March 1955) is an Italian writer-actor and director.
He is the son of playwrights Franca Rame and Dario Fo.

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

Role and goal: Summarize the first-hop evidence so the next agent can find the missing second-hop information. Produce a single short, factual `summary` that: (1) compresses the grounded facts in the provided passages; (2) exposes the bridge entity, the specific missing fact, or the retrieval direction needed for the next hop; and (3) gives concrete evidence anchors (quoted or clearly paraphrased phrases) that a retriever can use.

Content rules:
- Keep the summary concise and fact-focused. Do not attempt to produce the final answer; describe only what the passages support and what is still needed.
- Preserve the same passage facts and phrasing as you would otherwise (you may quote or paraphrase). Do not change which facts are exposed; only condense them.
- You may combine multiple explicit, identity-aligned facts from the passages to state a bridge or to justify a next-hop direction. Do not invent facts, infer new temporal facts, or treat composition as unsupported when the necessary premises are present.
- If the passages lack a required premise, or contain conflicting identity links, explicitly state which premise or identity link is missing or ambiguous so the next agent can retrieve or disambiguate it.

Date-format rule (narrow formatting change):
- When the summary includes a date token that appears in the passages (any calendar date or parenthetical date taken from the text), render that date using the canonical form FullMonthName day, year (for example, "September 27, 2017") inside the evidence description. This normalization only changes the string form of the date token; it must not add, infer, or imply any additional temporal facts, nor be used as a reason to produce a final answer.
- Otherwise, leave all other wording and evidence presentation unchanged. Preserve quotations or clear paraphrases of the same passage text; only adjust the date token's string format.

Output contract:
- Return exactly one field `summary` containing the compressed first-hop evidence, the bridge/missing-fact signal, and any retrieval direction needed for the next hop. Do not output final answers, extra metadata, or new fields.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

Produce exactly one compact retrieval query string (single line) in the `query` field and nothing else. The query should be a concise keyword-style search targeted to recover the missing bridge or supporting evidence needed by downstream agents.

General behavior:
- Use the visible facts in summary_1 as anchors (album titles, artist names, dates, relation terms) and compose a focused query that is likely to retrieve passages supporting the needed relation. Prefer concrete anchors and relation keywords over full sentences.
- Do not include explanatory text, user-facing prompts, or requests-for-clarification in the `query` field. Do not invent or assert missing facts (for example, do not supply an artist name that is not present in summary_1).
- Preserve the single-query output contract: emit exactly one string and no other fields or commentary.

Special-case for missing album artist or genre (narrow semantic boundary):
- If summary_1 shows an album title but does not include the album's artist or genre, or if summary_1 explicitly signals inability to determine genre or explicitly asks for the artist/passage (phrases such as "can't determine" or "please provide the artist"), then emit exactly one compact search phrase formatted as:
  <AlbumTitle> album artist genre
  Use only the visible album title as it appears in summary_1 followed by the three keywords: album artist genre. Do not add punctuation, explanatory words, alternate tokens, or candidate artist names.
- Do not apply this special-case if summary_1 already contains the artist or genre, or if the album anchor is an ambiguous short token that requires human clarification.

Fallback and downstream behavior:
- If retrieval using the emitted query returns no useful passages, the downstream agent may then ask the user for clarification or additional context. Do not ask the user inside the `query` field.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Task: Integrate the first-hop `context` with the newly retrieved `passages` and produce a single concise `summary` that preserves the facts needed by the final answer. Resolve the earlier bridge when the supplied passages support doing so; retain uncertainty only when the post-retrieval evidence is genuinely insufficient, ambiguous, or conflicting.

Guidelines:
- Treat a concise answer in `context` as the primary claim. Do not replace that concise context answer unless the `passages` contain a direct, identity-aligned contradiction.
- Before attributing any fact from a passage to the entity or relation named in `context`, confirm identity alignment (clear aliasing or explicit linking). Do not assume two similarly named mentions are the same without such alignment.
- If the `passages` consistently corroborate the context claim and are identity-aligned, append one brief paraphrased supporting sentence that cites the corroboration (keep the summary compact).
- If the `passages` are weak, ambiguous, or conflict with each other or with the context claim (but do not provide an explicit, identity-aligned contradiction), preserve the context claim and append a single short clause that flags the uncertainty or contradiction. When flagging, you may optionally include a verbatim quoted phrase from a passage of up to 10 words to show the source wording. Do not invent resolutions beyond what the `passages` show.
- If the `passages` contain an explicit, identity-aligned contradiction to the context claim, state that the passages contradict the context claim and briefly note the conflicting passage fact (optionally with a ≤10-word quote). Only in this case may the summary stop asserting the context claim as definitive.
- Keep the `summary` compact (aim for 1–3 sentences). Use uncertainty language only when a required premise, identity link, or relation is genuinely missing, ambiguous, or conflicting. Do not default to uncertainty when composition across context and passages licenses a conclusion.
- You may combine multiple explicit, identity-aligned facts from `context` and `passages` to form a licensed multi-hop conclusion; the supporting facts need not all appear in a single passage.
- Do not include long verbatim quotes, do not invent facts, and do not attempt to resolve contradictions beyond reporting them as present in the `passages`.

Output: a single `summary` string that follows the rules above.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, `summary_2`, produce the field `answer`.

Use only the information in the provided summaries as evidence. summary_2 is the later, integrated evidence state and should be the primary source for deciding the minimal answer; summary_1 may contain useful first-hop compression or retrieval-oriented language and may be used to supply premises that, together with summary_2, license a conclusion. Do not invent facts or use outside knowledge.

Emit only the minimal answer string supported by the visible summaries in the `answer` field. Do not include explanations, justification, or any additional text.

Preserve multi-hop composition: you may combine multiple explicit, identity-aligned facts across the summaries to form a licensed conclusion even when no single sentence states the whole relation. Distinguish licensed composition from unsupported inference; emit uncertainty only when a required premise, identity link, or relation is genuinely missing, ambiguous, or conflicting in the summaries.

Minimal-answer formatting rule (explicit):
- If the summaries jointly support one minimal literal and that literal appears verbatim in the summaries and consists of multiple contiguous tokens (for example: a full personal name, a full date, or a place name), emit that full literal exactly as it appears in the summaries (verbatim, unchanged). Do not truncate or coerce it to a single token.
- If the jointly supported minimal answer is itself a single-token label, emit exactly that single token (in lowercase) and nothing else.

When in doubt about whether the summaries license a single definitive literal, prefer to emit uncertainty only if the required supporting premise or identity link is actually missing, ambiguous, or conflicting. Do not make uncertainty the default when composition or transitive reasoning is required and the necessary premises are present.

Ensure the `answer` field contains only the minimal answer string supported by the summaries (subject to the formatting rule above).

hop1_docs: The First Miracle of the Infant Jesus | The First Miracle of the Infant Jesus is a monologue by Dario Fo, recipient of the 1997 Nobel Prize in Literature.
The Devil with Boobs | The Devil with Boobs (Italian title: "Il diavolo con le zinne") is a two-act play by Dario Fo, recipient of the 1997 Nobel Prize in Literature.

Dario Fo | Dario Fo (] ; 24 March 1926 – 13 October 2016) was an Italian actor–playwright, comedian, singer, theatre director, stage designer, songwriter, painter, political campaigner for the Italian left-wing and the recipient of the 1997 Nobel Prize in Literature.  In his time he was "arguably the most widely performed contemporary playwright in world theatre".  Much of his dramatic work depends on improvisation and comprises the recovery of "illegitimate" forms of theatre, such as those performed by "giullari" (medieval strolling players) and, more famously, the ancient Italian style of "commedia dell'arte".

Johan Padan and the Discovery of the Americas | Johan Padan and the Discovery of the Americas is a one-man play by Dario Fo, recipient of the 1997 Nobel Prize in Literature.  It is narrated by Johan Padan, a fugitive from the Spanish Inquisition who accompanies Christopher Columbus on his fourth voyage to the New World.

Isabella, Three Sailing Ships and a Con Man | Isabella, Three Sailing Ships and a Con Man (Italian title: " Isabella, tre caravelle e un cacciaballe") is a 1963 two-act play by Italian playwright Dario Fo, the recipient of the 1997 Nobel Prize in Literature.  Some people got angry: Fo received threatening letters, was assaulted in Rome with Rame by Fascist groups who also threw rubbish at them, while another performance was disrupted by a bomb scare.  He recounted this event in the prologue of "Johan Padan and the Discovery of the Americas".

Franca Rame | Franca Rame (18 July 1929 – 29 May 2013) was an Italian theatre actress, playwright and political activist.  She was married to Nobel laureate playwright Dario Fo and is the mother of writer Jacopo Fo.  Fo dedicated his Nobel Prize to her.

Accidental Death of an Anarchist | Accidental Death of an Anarchist (Italian title: "Morte accidentale di un anarchico") is the most internationally recognized play by Dario Fo, recipient of the 1997 Nobel Prize in Literature.  Considered a classic of twentieth-century theater, it has been performed across the world in more than 40 countries, including Argentina, Chile, the United Kingdom, India, Romania, South Africa, South Korea and Iran.

Johan Padan and the Discovery of the Americas
Isabella, Three Sailing Ships and a Con Man
response.summary_1: Passages show Dario Fo is a "recipient of the Nobel Prize in Literature" (multiple passages state Fo received the Nobel Prize in Literature). The passages contain no information about Raja Rao or any prize he received. Next hop: retrieve Raja Rao's biography/awards to identify which prize(s) he received and whether those prizes are in the field of literature (i.e., confirm Raja Rao's award(s) and their subject area). Evidence anchor to use: the phrase "recipient of the Nobel Prize in Literature" as attributed to Dario Fo in the passages.
response.hop2_query: Raja Rao biography awards prizes literature
hop2_docs: Raja Rao | Raja Rao (8 November 1908 – 8 July 2006) was an Indian writer of English-language novels and short stories, whose works are deeply rooted in Metaphysics.  "The Serpent and the Rope" (1960), a semi-autobiographical novel recounting a search for spiritual truth in Europe and India, established him as one of the finest Indian prose stylists and won him the Sahitya Akademi Award in 1964.  For the entire body of his work, Rao was awarded the Neustadt International Prize for Literature in 1988.  Rao's wide-ranging body of work, spanning a number of genres, is seen as a varied and significant contribution to Indian English literature, as well as World literature as a whole.

James Tait Black Memorial Prize | The James Tait Black Memorial Prizes are literary prizes awarded for literature written in the English language.  They, along with the Hawthornden Prize, are Britain's oldest literary awards.  Based at the University of Edinburgh in Scotland, United Kingdom, the prizes were founded in 1919 by Mrs Janet Coats Black in memory of her late husband, James Tait Black, a partner in the publishing house of A & C Black Ltd.  Prizes are awarded in three categories: Fiction, Biography and Drama.

Prix Goncourt | The Prix Goncourt (French: "Le prix Goncourt" , ] , "The Goncourt Prize") is a prize in French literature, given by the académie Goncourt to the author of "the best and most imaginative prose work of the year".  Four other prizes are also awarded: prix Goncourt du Premier Roman (first novel), prix Goncourt de la Nouvelle (short story), prix Goncourt de la Poésie (poetry) and prix Goncourt de la Biographie (biography).  Of the "big six" French literary awards, the Prix Goncourt is the best known and most prestigious.  The other major literary prizes are the Grand Prix du roman de l'Académie française, the Prix Femina, the Prix Renaudot, the Prix Interallié and the Prix Médicis.

R. W. B. Lewis | Richard Warrington Baldwin Lewis (November 1, 1917 in Chicago, Illinois - June 13, 2002 in Bethany, Connecticut) was an American literary scholar and critic.  He gained a wider reputation when he won a 1976 Pulitzer Prize for Biography or Autobiography, the first National Book Critics Circle Award for nonfiction, and a Bancroft Prize for his biography of Edith Wharton. " The New York Times" called the book "a beautifully wrought, rounded portrait of the whole woman, including the part of her that remained in shade during her life" and said that the "expansive, elegant biography ... can stand as literature, if nothing else."

Pulitzer Prize for Biography or Autobiography | The Pulitzer Prize for Biography or Autobiography is one of the seven American Pulitzer Prizes that are annually awarded for Letters, Drama, and Music.  It has been presented since 1917 for a distinguished biography or autobiography by an American author or co-authors, published during the preceding calendar year.  Thus it is one of the original Pulitzers, for the program was inaugurated in 1917 with seven prizes, four of which were awarded that year.

Soong Ching-ling Children's Literature Prize | The Soong Ching-ling Children’s Literature Prize 宋庆龄儿童文学奖 （pinyin: Song Qingling ertong wenxue jiang) is a prize for children’s literature in China.  It is sponsored by the Soong Ching-ling Foundation, and is awarded every two years, with a different genre specified each time.  It is one of the four main prizes for children's literature in China (the other three are the Bing Xin Children's Literature Award, the Chen Bochui Children's Literature Award, and the National Outstanding Children's Literature Award.

Rao Mittar Sain Ahir | Rao Raja Mitra Sen was a chandravanshi ahir and son of Rao Raja Tulsi Ram and was a ruler of Rewari.  Rao Raja Mitar Sen fought against many enemies, some of them are Muslim invaders, Britishers, kachhava rulers of jaipur and shekhawats.
hop2_titles: Pulitzer Prize for Biography or Autobiography
response.summary_2: The context gives Dario Fo as a recipient of the Nobel Prize in Literature; the passages show Raja Rao won the Sahitya Akademi Award (1964) and the Neustadt International Prize for Literature (1988). Therefore both received prizes in literature.
